mnemonics.title: PTE FIELDS MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: P.T.E. V.D.R.P: Protection bits, Time (aging), Entry valid/invalid, Dirty bit, Reference bit, Physical frame number

mnemonics.title: PAGE FAULT STEPS
mnemonics.content: T.R.A.P.S: Trap to OS, Reference check, Allocate frame, Page in from disk, Set PTE, Restart instruction

mnemonics.title: TLB BENEFITS
mnemonics.content: FAST: Fast translation, Avoids page table access, Speeds memory, Time saved

acronyms.usage: Individual entry in page table containing translation and status information
Hardware cache for storing recent address translations
Logical address generated by CPU, needs translation to physical address
Actual address in physical memory where data is stored
Portion of virtual address used to index page table
Physical page number identifying the frame in RAM

visualTips.visualization: Draw virtual address as three boxes: page index, frame index, and offset. Show arrows from page index to PTE, then to frame number, and combine with offset to form physical address.
Visualize as a tree: root node (outer page table) branches to intermediate nodes (inner page tables), each pointing to leaf pages. Only branches with valid pages are filled in.
Show a grid representing physical memory frames as rows. Each row shows which process and virtual page occupies that frame, indexed by frame number rather than virtual address.
Show parallel comparison: virtual page number compared against all TLB entries simultaneously (like searching multiple locks at once), with match producing instant physical frame.

analogies.analogy: Like a book index: virtual page number is like a topic, page table entry tells you which physical page (actual book page) contains that information.
Like a dictionary bookmark: instead of flipping through pages every time, you keep frequently used word-page mappings handy for instant reference.
Like requesting a book from the library: the book (page) isn't in your hands (memory), so a request is made to fetch it, then you can read it.
Like a university directory: main directory (outer table) points to department listings (inner tables), which point to specific offices (pages), only allocating space for actual departments.
Like a document's unsaved changes indicator: if you've made edits (modified = 1), you must save to disk before closing; if no edits (modified = 0), you can discard without saving.
